items.q: How long does the ATP-PC system last at maximum intensity?
items.why: Students overestimate the ATP-PC system. It provides instant energy but stores deplete in ~10 seconds (one sprint, one heavy lift). After that, glycolysis or aerobic systems take over.

items.q: Which energy system fuels a marathon?
items.why: Students associate hard exercise with anaerobic systems. Long-duration steady-state work uses AEROBIC metabolism (oxygen + carbs/fats) - efficient but slower to ramp up. Anaerobic systems dominate brief intense efforts.

items.q: What causes muscle soreness 1-2 days after intense exercise?
items.correct: Microscopic muscle damage (DOMS), not lactic acid.
items.misconception: Lactic acid still in the muscles.
items.why: Students believe the myth that lactic acid causes soreness. Lactate clears within an hour. Delayed onset muscle soreness (DOMS) is caused by microtrauma from eccentric contractions, peaking 24-72 hours later.

items.q: The rate-limiting enzyme of glycolysis is:
items.why: It's Phosphofructokinase. “Hexokinase” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Fatigue in anaerobic glycolysis primarily reflects:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: H⁺ accumulation. “Lactate itself” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If anaerobic glycolysis yields 2 ATP per glucose and aerobic yields 32, anaerobic efficiency relative to aerobic (%) is?
items.why: The real figure is 6. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
